context: Before installing OpenShift GitOps on an OpenShift cluster
desired_outcome: Ensure adequate cluster resources are allocated to prevent installation failures and pod scheduling issues
success_criteria: Resource quotas configured correctly
All GitOps workload pods can be scheduled
Redis pod has sufficient memory for large-scale deployments
section: Preparing to install OpenShift GitOps > Sizing requirements for GitOps
evidence: Lines 5-78: Details resource requirements for all GitOps workloads including CPU/memory requests and limits
pain_points: Default Redis memory limits insufficient for large deployments
Installation failures when namespace resource quotas conflict with operator requirements
Difficulty estimating capacity needs without documentation
alternatives: Install without planning and troubleshoot resource issues reactively
Use default settings and scale up resources only when problems occur
notes: Covers default Argo CD instance sizing and Redis-specific capacity planning. Includes modification procedures via ArgoCD CR.

user_story: When planning GitOps installation, I want to understand default resource allocations for all workload components, so I can verify my cluster has sufficient capacity
desired_outcome: Know exact CPU and memory requests/limits for all 7 GitOps workload components
success_criteria: Can compare workload requirements against available cluster resources
Understand which components consume most resources
Know how to view and modify resource settings via ArgoCD CR
section: Preparing to install OpenShift GitOps > Sizing requirements for GitOps
evidence: Lines 11-30: Table showing CPU/memory requests and limits for 7 workloads, plus `oc edit argocd` command
pain_points: Need to manually sum resources across all components
Resource requirements table doesn't indicate total footprint
alternatives: Install first and check actual resource usage afterward
notes: Provides detailed table with 7 workload components: application-controller, applicationset-controller, server, repo-server, redis, dex-server, redis-ha-haproxy

user_story: When managing large-scale GitOps deployments, I want to increase Redis memory limits beyond defaults, so the pod can handle increased resource counts without failures
context: Capacity planning for applications with many resources or scaling up existing deployment
desired_outcome: Redis pod configured with adequate memory (e.g., 8Gi limit, 256Mi request) for large deployments
success_criteria: Successfully patch ArgoCD CR to increase Redis memory
Verify new limits applied to redis pod
Monitor memory metrics during application scale-up
section: Preparing to install OpenShift GitOps > Sizing requirements for Argo CD redis
evidence: Lines 32-78: Explains default Redis limits may be insufficient, shows how to check current config with jq, and provides oc patch command to set 8Gi limit
pain_points: Default 256Mi memory limit insufficient for large resource counts
Need to monitor metrics and adjust reactively during scale-up
Must use JSON patch syntax which is error-prone
alternatives: Accept default limits and restart pods when memory issues occur
Pre-configure higher limits in initial ArgoCD CR before installation
dependencies: jq installed for viewing current config
notes: Includes commands to view current Redis resource config with jq and patch command to set custom limits/requests

main_job: Install GitOps Operator via web console
context: Need to enable GitOps capabilities on an OpenShift cluster using the web console UI
desired_outcome: GitOps Operator successfully installed with default Argo CD instance available in openshift-gitops namespace
success_criteria: Operator status shows Succeeded in Installed Operators
Default Argo CD instance created in openshift-gitops namespace
Argo CD icon appears in console toolbar
All GitOps pods running successfully
section: Installing OpenShift GitOps > Installing OpenShift GitOps Operator in web console
evidence: Lines 104-151: Complete web console installation procedure from OperatorHub search through verification
pain_points: Must remove Community Argo CD Operator if already installed (conflicts)
Namespace change in v1.10+ from openshift-operators to openshift-gitops-operator may confuse users
Need to understand channel vs version selection
alternatives: Use CLI installation method instead
Use GitOps Operator as part of cluster install via IPI
blockers: Marketplace capability or manual catalog source configuration required
Cannot have Community Argo CD Operator installed
notes: Web console method is simpler than CLI but less automatable. Automatically creates ready-to-use Argo CD instance.

main_job: Install GitOps Operator via web console
user_story: When installing GitOps Operator from web console, I want to select the appropriate update channel and version, so I can control which operator version is installed
context: On the Install Operator page in OperatorHub
desired_outcome: Correct operator version installed via chosen channel (e.g., gitops-1.19 for v1.19.x, or latest for most recent stable)
success_criteria: Understand difference between 'latest' channel and version-specific channels
Selected channel matches organization's version requirements
Installation proceeds without channel/version conflicts
section: Installing OpenShift GitOps > Installing OpenShift GitOps Operator in web console
evidence: Lines 97-102: Explanation that 'latest' channel installs most recent stable version, and version-specific channels like 'gitops-1.19' available for specific versions
pain_points: 'Latest' channel may auto-upgrade to new major versions unexpectedly
Unclear which channel to use for production vs non-production clusters
alternatives: Use CLI installation to specify channel in subscription YAML
Accept defaults and upgrade manually later if needed
notes: Channel selection affects upgrade behavior. Version-specific channels provide more control but require manual channel changes for upgrades.

main_job: Install GitOps Operator via web console
user_story: When installing GitOps Operator, I want to enable cluster monitoring for the operator namespace, so I can track operator performance and health
context: Configuring installation options in web console
desired_outcome: Cluster monitoring enabled for openshift-gitops-operator namespace via checkbox or label
success_criteria: Cluster monitoring checkbox selected during installation
Can verify namespace has openshift.io/cluster-monitoring=true label
Operator metrics available in cluster monitoring
section: Installing OpenShift GitOps > Installing OpenShift GitOps Operator in web console
evidence: Lines 127-143: Instructions to enable cluster monitoring via checkbox or apply openshift.io/cluster-monitoring=true label to any namespace
pain_points: Monitoring not enabled by default
Can forget to enable during installation and have to apply label afterward
alternatives: Enable monitoring after installation via oc label command
Skip monitoring and rely on operator logs only
notes: Same monitoring enablement approach works for web console (checkbox) and CLI (label) installations

context: Need to install GitOps Operator using command-line tools for automation or when web console unavailable
desired_outcome: GitOps Operator installed via CLI with namespace, OperatorGroup, and Subscription configured correctly
success_criteria: openshift-gitops-operator namespace created
OperatorGroup created successfully
Subscription created and operator installation succeeds
All pods running in openshift-gitops and openshift-gitops-operator namespaces
section: Installing OpenShift GitOps > Installing OpenShift GitOps Operator using CLI
evidence: Lines 153-296: Complete CLI installation procedure including namespace creation, OperatorGroup, Subscription, and verification steps
pain_points: Requires creating and applying multiple YAML files (OperatorGroup, Subscription)
Namespace default changed in v1.10+ which may break existing automation
Must understand Subscription YAML structure and field meanings
alternatives: Use web console installation method
Use GitOps to install GitOps (bootstrap problem)
blockers: Marketplace capability or catalog source required
notes: CLI method enables automation and is repeatable. Includes detailed verification steps for both namespaces.

user_story: When creating Subscription for GitOps Operator, I want to understand the YAML structure and field meanings, so I can customize the installation appropriately
context: Creating openshift-gitops-sub.yaml file for operator subscription
desired_outcome: Valid Subscription YAML with correct channel, source, and namespace configuration
success_criteria: Understand role of metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.channel, spec.source fields

Subscription applies successfully without errors
section: Installing OpenShift GitOps > Installing OpenShift GitOps Operator using CLI
evidence: Lines 225-248: Example Subscription YAML with field-by-field explanation of metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.channel, spec.source, sourceNamespace
pain_points: Field descriptions have typos (e.g., 'specify the specify')

alternatives: Copy example YAML without understanding fields
Use web console to avoid YAML creation
notes: Subscription controls operator updates via channel and installPlanApproval fields

user_story: When installing GitOps Operator via CLI, I want to enable cluster monitoring for the operator namespace, so I can track operator performance
desired_outcome: Cluster monitoring enabled via openshift.io/cluster-monitoring=true label
success_criteria: Label applied successfully to namespace
Can verify monitoring metrics available for operator
section: Installing OpenShift GitOps > Installing OpenShift GitOps Operator using CLI
evidence: Lines 182-196: Instructions to apply openshift.io/cluster-monitoring=true label to enable cluster monitoring on namespace
pain_points: Must remember to apply label separately after namespace creation
Monitoring not enabled by default
alternatives: Enable monitoring after installation
Skip monitoring and rely on logs only
notes: Same label approach works for any namespace, not just operator namespace

user_story: When GitOps Operator installation completes, I want to verify all expected pods are running, so I can confirm successful installation
context: After applying Subscription and waiting for operator installation
desired_outcome: All GitOps pods running in both openshift-gitops and openshift-gitops-operator namespaces
success_criteria: 8 pods running in openshift-gitops namespace (cluster, gitops-plugin, application-controller, applicationset-controller, dex-server, redis, repo-server, server)
2 pods running in openshift-gitops-operator namespace (controller-manager)
All pod statuses show Running with appropriate READY counts
section: Installing OpenShift GitOps > Installing OpenShift GitOps Operator using CLI
evidence: Lines 263-296: Two verification commands (oc get pods in both namespaces) with example output showing expected pod names and statuses
pain_points: Must check two separate namespaces
Pod names include generated suffixes which change between installations
Need to understand which pods should be in which namespace
alternatives: Only check operator namespace and assume gitops namespace is fine
Use web console to view pod status visually
notes: Example output shows specific pod counts and naming patterns to expect

main_job: Access Argo CD UI after installation
context: After installing GitOps Operator, need to log into Argo CD UI to begin managing applications
desired_outcome: Successfully logged into Argo CD UI using either OpenShift credentials or admin password
success_criteria: Can access Argo CD UI via console link or direct URL
Successfully authenticate with cluster-admin OpenShift credentials or admin password
Argo CD dashboard loads and shows ready-to-use instance
section: Installing OpenShift GitOps > Logging in to the Argo CD instance by using the Argo CD admin account
evidence: Lines 298-333: Complete login procedure via web console navigation, optional OpenShift SSO, and admin password retrieval from secrets
pain_points: Admin password stored in secret requires multiple navigation steps to retrieve
Must be member of cluster-admins group for OpenShift SSO login
Cannot modify argocd-secret with external secret managers without causing conflicts
alternatives: Use argocd CLI to login instead of UI
Configure alternative authentication (SSO/OIDC) before accessing
blockers: Must be cluster-admin or member of cluster-admins group for OpenShift auth
notes: Default Argo CD instance automatically created in openshift-gitops namespace. Can create additional instances for applications.

main_job: Access Argo CD UI after installation
user_story: When logging into Argo CD for the first time, I want to retrieve the admin password from the cluster secret, so I can authenticate without SSO
context: Accessing Argo CD UI login page
desired_outcome: Admin password retrieved and used successfully to log into Argo CD UI
success_criteria: Navigate to Workloads > Secrets in correct namespace
Find and open <argo_CD_instance_name>-cluster secret
Copy admin.password value from Details tab
Successfully log in with username 'admin' and copied password
section: Installing OpenShift GitOps > Logging in to the Argo CD instance by using the Argo CD admin account
evidence: Lines 318-323: Step-by-step instructions to navigate to Secrets, select namespace, find instance secret, and copy admin.password field
pain_points: Multi-step navigation required to find password
Password is random and cannot be predicted
Secret name includes instance name which may vary
alternatives: Use OpenShift credentials if member of cluster-admins group
Retrieve password via oc get secret command from CLI
frequency: Once per instance (or when password reset)
blockers: Must have access to view secrets in namespace
notes: Admin password stored in Data > admin.password field of <instance>-cluster secret

main_job: Access Argo CD UI after installation
user_story: When accessing Argo CD UI, I want to use my OpenShift credentials via SSO, so I don't need to manage separate admin passwords
context: At Argo CD login page with LOG IN VIA OPENSHIFT option available
desired_outcome: Successfully authenticate using OpenShift cluster-admin credentials
success_criteria: Member of cluster-admins group in OpenShift

Authenticated and returned to Argo CD UI
section: Installing OpenShift GitOps > Logging in to the Argo CD instance by using the Argo CD admin account
evidence: Lines 312-317: Instructions to select LOG IN VIA OPENSHIFT option and requirement to be member of cluster-admins group
pain_points: Must be member of cluster-admins group (not just have cluster-admin role binding)
Need to run oc adm groups command to add users to cluster-admins group
alternatives: Use admin password from secret instead
Configure alternative RBAC groups in Argo CD
blockers: Must be member of cluster-admins group
notes: Requires cluster-admins group membership, not just cluster-admin role. Use 'oc adm groups new cluster-admins <user>' to add users.

main_job: Install GitOps CLI tool on workstation
context: Need command-line access to configure and manage GitOps and Argo CD resources
desired_outcome: GitOps argocd CLI installed and functional on local workstation (Linux, macOS, or Windows)
success_criteria: Binary downloaded for correct OS and architecture
Binary installed in PATH location
Executable permissions set (Linux/macOS)
CLI version command succeeds and shows GitOps version info
evidence: Lines 335-636: Complete installation procedures for Linux (tar.gz and RPM), Windows (zip), and macOS (tar.gz) with verification steps
pain_points: Technology Preview status means not supported in production
Different installation methods per OS
RPM method requires active RHEL subscription and subscription-manager setup
alternatives: Use Argo CD web UI instead of CLI
Use kubectl/oc directly without argocd CLI wrapper
blockers: Must have appropriate OS permissions to install binaries
dependencies: Internet access to download from content gateway
For RPM: Active OCP subscription and RHEL 8+
notes: CLI is Technology Preview. Archives and RPMs both contain same argocd executable. Newer CLI versions compatible with older GitOps server.

main_job: Install GitOps CLI tool on workstation

Use pre-existing argocd binary if compatible version available
notes: Supports 4 Linux architectures: x86_64/amd64, s390x (IBM Z), ppc64le (IBM Power), aarch64/arm64

success_criteria: Registered with Red Hat Subscription Manager

Installed openshift-gitops-argocd-cli package
argocd version --client shows expected output

evidence: Lines 419-517: Complete subscription-manager workflow (register, refresh, list, attach, enable repos) plus yum install and verification
pain_points: Complex multi-step subscription-manager workflow required
Must know correct repo name format with version and architecture variables

Need root/sudo privileges
alternatives: Use tar.gz method to avoid subscription-manager setup

notes: RPM method enables automatic updates via dnf upgrade. Supports 4 architectures: x86_64, s390x, ppc64le, aarch64
